title: Hassabis Admits He Would Have Kept AI in the Lab Longer — And That Matters

source.original: The Demis Hassabis HUGE Conversation (in full) — Cleo Abram

body_html:
In a wide-ranging conversation with Cleo Abram, Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis made a striking admission: he would have preferred to keep AI in the lab longer before releasing it to the public. Coming from the head of one of the world's most advanced AI organizations, this isn't idle philosophizing — it's a window into the internal tensions shaping how frontier AI gets deployed.

The statement reflects a growing unease among top AI researchers that competitive pressure — the fear of being outpaced by rivals — is driving premature releases of increasingly powerful systems. Hassabis occupies a unique position as both a scientific visionary who helped build AlphaFold and a Google executive whose employer is locked in an intense AI race. That duality makes his candor especially significant.

What does it mean for the broader AI landscape when a leading figure acknowledges the pace may be too fast? It suggests that even inside the labs building the most capable systems, there is recognition that governance, safety testing, and societal readiness have not kept up with the technology. The gap between what AI can do and what institutions are prepared to handle continues to widen.

This admission also complicates the narrative that AI companies are uniformly bullish on rapid deployment. Hassabis's words point to a more nuanced reality where the people closest to the technology harbor genuine reservations about the speed at which it is being released — reservations that may not always survive the pressures of quarterly targets and competitive dynamics.
